nativeRange.habitat: Steep outer reef slopes and occasionally lagoon reefs, generally at depths of around 5–45 m (records to 90 m); typically associated with coral-rich and rubble zones

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on filamentous algae, making it functionally herbivorous. In captivity, generally advised to offer a varied diet including spirulina, dried marine algae (nori), high-quality angelfish/herbivore preparations, mysis shrimp, and finely chopped meaty foods. Feeding small amounts 2–3 times daily is commonly recommended, and substantial live rock for grazing tends to support long-term health.

avoidWith: very passive or slow-moving fish that may be harassed

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by seastung; 640x428. Sharp, well-lit lateral shot of the whole fish; bright yellow with the diagnostic dusky patch behind the eye and no blue eye rings, confirming Centropyge heraldi over the similar Lemonpeel.

Photo by kfa; 2048x1536. In-situ reef shot showing the whole bright-yellow fish in natural habitat; correctly ID'd but the fish is small in frame with a blue color cast, so kept as a secondary in-situ example.

summary: The Yellow Angelfish (Centropyge heraldi) is a uniformly bright yellow dwarf angelfish from the Western and Central Pacific. It is distinguished from the similar Lemonpeel Angelfish by the absence of blue rings around the eyes and gill cover, and by a small dusky olive patch behind the eye in adults. Generally considered semi-aggressive and reef-safe with caution.
careNotes: The Yellow Angelfish, also widely sold as Herald's Angelfish or False Lemonpeel Angelfish, is most readily distinguished from the true Lemonpeel (Centropyge flavissima) by the absence of blue rings around the eyes and gill cover, along with a small dusky olive patch behind the eye in adults. It is commonly considered an intermediate-level dwarf angelfish, one that tends to do best in a well-established aquarium with substantial live rock for grazing. It has been reported to nip at large-polyped stony corals (LPS — those with bigger, fleshy feeding tentacles), soft coral polyps, and clam mantles, making it generally regarded as reef-safe with caution rather than fully reef-safe. The Yellow Angelfish is best kept singly; introducing two individuals of the same genus into the same aquarium tends to result in persistent aggression. This species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite and forms small harems in the wild, typically one dominant male with one to three females. Females tend to transition to male in the absence of a dominant male, consistent with other Centropyge angelfishes.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been reported but is considered challenging and rarely attempted outside specialist facilities. The Yellow Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite that forms small harems in the wild; spawning tends to occur in the evening as broadcast pelagic spawning, with eggs and sperm released into the water column. Visual sexing is not straightforward. Larval rearing is technically demanding.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (12.0 cm TL, consistent with the upper end), taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes), native range (Indo-Pacific from Sri Lanka/Bali through to the Marshall Islands; Wikipedia extends to Tuamotus and Great Barrier Reef), depth range (5–90 m), preferred temperature (25–29°C, mean 27.6°C — averaged with LiveAquaria 22–26°C and Reeflings 24–28°C to give final 23–27°C), feeding ecology (filamentous algae, herbivorous in the wild), and harem structure (2–4 individuals).

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Third source used to corroborate adult size (~10 cm / 4 in), tank size (200 L / 53 gal — averaged with LiveAquaria's 70 gal / ~265 L and biased larger to 300 L for territoriality and rockwork), temperature (24–28°C), salinity (SG 1.023–1.026, averaged with LiveAquaria's 1.020–1.025 to give 1.021–1.025), reef-safe rating (with caution), and lifespan (5–7 years; extended slightly to 5–8 to reflect well-kept individuals).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used as taxonomy fallback for native range (Western and Central Pacific to Great Barrier Reef and Tuamotus), depth (8–45 m), confirmation of protogynous hermaphroditism (females transition to males when no dominant male present), and harem structure (1 male + 1–3 females).

fish.breedingNotes: The Yellow Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite and a harem spawner in the wild, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column as broadcast (pelagic) spawning. Captive breeding has been reported but is rarely achieved outside specialist facilities.
